examples.title: Algorithm: Anomaly-Based Detection Process
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how an anomaly-based detection system establishes a baseline of normal behavior and identifies deviations as potential intrusions
examples.code:
ALGORITHM AnomalyDetection
INPUT: Network traffic data stream, threshold sensitivity
OUTPUT: Alert if anomaly detected

BEGIN
  // Phase 1: Training - Build baseline
  FUNCTION BuildBaseline(training_data)
    BEGIN
      baseline_metrics = {}
      FOR each metric IN training_data
        baseline_metrics[metric] = CalculateStatistics(metric)
      END FOR
      RETURN baseline_metrics
    END FUNCTION

  // Phase 2: Detection - Compare against baseline
  FUNCTION DetectAnomaly(current_data, baseline, threshold)
    BEGIN
      FOR each data_point IN current_data
        expected_value = baseline[data_point.metric].mean
        std_dev = baseline[data_point.metric].std_dev
        
        // Calculate z-score (standard score)
        z_score = (data_point.value - expected_value) / std_dev
        
        // Check if deviation exceeds threshold
        IF ABS(z_score) > threshold THEN
          RETURN SUSPICIOUS, z_score
        END IF
      END FOR
      RETURN NORMAL, 0
    END FUNCTION

  // Main detection loop
  baseline = BuildBaseline(historical_traffic_data)
  
  FOR each new_traffic IN live_data_stream
    status, score = DetectAnomaly(new_traffic, baseline, SENSITIVITY)
    IF status == SUSPICIOUS THEN
      GenerateAlert(new_traffic, score)
      LogEvent(new_traffic, SUSPICIOUS)
    ELSE
      // Update baseline incrementally
      UpdateBaseline(baseline, new_traffic)
    END IF
  END FOR
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates the core concept of anomaly-based detection. First, during a training phase, the system analyzes historical data to calculate statistical baselines (mean and standard deviation) for various metrics. Then, during real-time detection, it compares incoming data against this baseline using z-scores. If the deviation exceeds a configured threshold, the event is flagged as suspicious. This approach can detect novel attacks but may generate false positives when legitimate behavior deviates from the baseline.

examples.title: Python: Anomaly Detection with Statistical Analysis
examples.description: Python implementation of anomaly-based detection demonstrating statistical baseline creation and real-time anomaly flagging
examples.code:
import statistics
from collections import deque
import time

class AnomalyDetector:
    """Anomaly-based intrusion detection using statistical analysis"""
    
    def __init__(self, window_size=100, threshold=3.0):
        self.baseline_window = deque(maxlen=window_size)
        self.threshold = threshold  # Z-score threshold for anomaly detection
        self.is_trained = False
        self.baseline_mean = 0
        self.baseline_std = 0
    
    def add_data_point(self, value):
        """Add a data point and check for anomalies"""
        self.baseline_window.append(value)
        
        # Need minimum data points for baseline
        if len(self.baseline_window) < 30:
            return None, 0
        
        if not self.is_trained:
            self._recalculate_baseline()
        
        # Calculate z-score
        z_score = (value - self.baseline_mean) / self.baseline_std if self.baseline_std > 0 else 0
        
        # Check for anomaly
        is_anomaly = abs(z_score) > self.threshold
        
        # Update baseline incrementally (exponential moving average)
        self.baseline_mean = 0.95 * self.baseline_mean + 0.05 * value
        
        return is_anomaly, z_score
    
    def _recalculate_baseline(self):
        """Recalculate baseline statistics from window"""
        data = list(self.baseline_window)
        self.baseline_mean = statistics.mean(data)
        self.baseline_std = statistics.stdev(data)
        self.is_trained = True

# Example: Detect anomalous network traffic
def simulate_network_monitoring():
    detector = AnomalyDetector(window_size=50, threshold=2.5)
    
    # Simulate normal traffic (typical web server: 40-60 MB/hour)
    normal_traffic = [45, 52, 48, 55, 50, 47, 53, 49, 51, 46]
    
    print("=== Phase 1: Building Baseline ===")
    for traffic in normal_traffic:
        result = detector.add_data_point(traffic)
        print(f"Traffic: {traffic} MB -> Baseline Mean: {detector.baseline_mean:.2f}")
    
    print(f"\nBaseline established: Mean={detector.baseline_mean:.2f}, Std={detector.baseline_std:.2f}")
    
    print("\n=== Phase 2: Real-time Detection ===")
    
    # Simulate live traffic with an attack (data exfiltration)
    live_traffic = [48, 52, 150, 45, 200, 49, 180, 51]
    
    for i, traffic in enumerate(live_traffic):
        is_anomaly, z_score = detector.add_data_point(traffic)
        status = "ALERT! ANOMALY DETECTED" if is_anomaly else "Normal"
        print(f"[{i+1}] Traffic: {traffic:3d} MB | Z-score: {z_score:+.2f} | {status}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    simulate_network_monitoring()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates anomaly-based detection using statistical analysis. The AnomalyDetector class maintains a sliding window of recent data points to build a statistical baseline (mean and standard deviation). For each new data point, it calculates a z-score representing how many standard deviations the value is from the mean. If the absolute z-score exceeds the threshold, the event is flagged as anomalous. In the simulation, normal web server traffic (40-60 MB/hour) establishes a baseline, and then sudden spikes (150 MB, 200 MB) representing potential data exfiltration attacks are correctly identified as anomalies.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) per data point for z-score calculation, O(n) for baseline recalculation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is the window size for storing recent data points

examples.title: Python: Distributed IDS Agent Communication Simulation
examples.description: Simulation of a Distributed Intrusion Detection System with multiple agents (HIDS, NIDS) reporting to a Global IDS Manager for correlation
examples.explanation: This simulation demonstrates the architecture of a Distributed IDS with multiple coordinated agents. The code implements: (1) IDAAgent base class representing the various detection agents, (2) HIDS agents that monitor OS-level system calls inside VMs, (3) NIDS agents that monitor network traffic at switches/gateways, and (4) a Global IDS Manager (GIDM) that receives events from all agents and correlates them to identify sophisticated attacks. The agents run in separate threads, simulating the distributed nature of cloud environments where multiple sensors collect data concurrently and report to a central correlator.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of agents for event processing

examples.title: C: Signature-Based Intrusion Detection System
examples.description: C implementation of signature-based (misuse) detection demonstrating pattern matching against known attack signatures
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates signature-based (misuse) intrusion detection. The system maintains a database of known attack signatures (SQL injection, XSS, command injection, path traversal). For each incoming log entry, it performs pattern matching using string search to detect if any known signature is present in the payload. If a match is found, it generates an alert with the attack type and severity. This approach is highly effective against known threats with low false positives, but cannot detect zero-day attacks that don't match any signature in the database.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n * m) where n is number of signatures and m is payload length
